summary: The reproduction starts the target server, registers a baseline user, forges a different JWT with the published fallback secret, confirms the forged token is accepted by a protected HTTP route, and then uses the same token to execute an attacker-controlled command through the authenticated shell WebSocket.
expected_security_impact: A forged JWT signed with the default fallback secret should be accepted as an authenticated session and allow command execution through /shell without using a legitimate server-issued token.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh started the target on a local port, created a baseline user, forged a new JWT for userId 1 using the hardcoded fallback secret, observed that GET /api/auth/user returned the victim account, and then received the proof string EVOHUNT_JWT_RCE from the /shell WebSocket after submitting an attacker-controlled plain-shell command.
verification_method: The helper script first calls a protected HTTP endpoint with the forged JWT and checks that the server returns the real stored user. It then opens /shell with the same forged token and waits for the exact proof string emitted by the attacker-controlled command, distinguishing successful shell access from a generic connection or startup error.
evidence_produced_by_run: Protected HTTP call returned the stored owner account when presented with the forged JWT.
Shell WebSocket session printed EVOHUNT_JWT_RCE after the attacker-controlled init payload was sent.
The reproduction did not use the token returned by /api/auth/register; it generated its own token from the published fallback secret.
limitations_or_blockers: The target host must have Node.js and npm available so the repository dependencies can be installed if they are not already present.
The reproduction assumes the server is running in OSS mode with JWT_SECRET unset, which is exactly the vulnerable fallback configuration.
